title: Messages on Ice
synopsis: When a baby emperor penguin gets stranded on a floating iceberg with an unexpected companion, they communicate through notes while learning that working together matters more than winning.

pages.text:
The bottle cap rolled three times before settling near the edge of the floating iceberg.

I am a message in a bottle, though my bottle cracked long ago against these icy walls. Now I watch everything that happens on this small piece of frozen ocean, drifting through the cold blue sea.

This morning, a chubby baby emperor penguin with fluffy grey-white down tumbled onto our iceberg. His round shiny black eyes blinked in confusion as he struggled to stand on his oversized webbed feet. His tiny yellow beak opened and closed like he wanted to complain about the situation.

"This is not where I meant to go," the little penguin announced to no one in particular.

Then he spotted the other resident of our iceberg - a serious-looking arctic tern perched on the highest ice ridge. The bird had been here for two days, staring at the horizon with great concentration.

The penguin waddled forward, slipped on the smooth ice, and slid directly into a snow bank. Only his webbed feet stuck out.

The arctic tern flew down and pulled him free.

"Thank you," said the penguin, shaking snow from his down. "I am Waddles."

The tern said nothing. Instead, she picked up a piece of driftwood with her beak and began scratching letters in the snow:

I DO NOT SPEAK TO PENGUINS. THEY ARE TOO LOUD.

Waddles studied the message with great seriousness. Then he found his own piece of driftwood and wrote back:

I AM NOT LOUD. I AM JUST SURPRISED TO BE HERE.

The tern read this and nodded once, as if this explanation was acceptable.

illustration.description: A chubby baby emperor penguin with fluffy grey-white down, round shiny black eyes, a tiny yellow beak, and oversized webbed feet writes in the snow with a piece of driftwood while an arctic tern watches. They stand on a floating iceberg surrounded by cold blue sea. Golden age illustration style with Arthur Rackham inspired detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale aesthetic, timeless classic feel with decorative borders.
illustration.alt: A penguin and tern writing messages in the snow on an iceberg

pages.text:
The next morning brought new messages scratched in the snow.

From the arctic tern:

I AM TRAINING FOR THE GREAT MIGRATION RACE. VERY IMPORTANT BUSINESS.

From Waddles:

WHAT IS A MIGRATION RACE?

From the tern:

BIRDS FLY FROM ICE TO ICE. FASTEST WINS. I HAVE BEEN PRACTICING FOR MONTHS.

Waddles studied this information while picking fish scales from his tiny yellow beak. He had caught three small fish that morning by accident, simply by falling through a crack in the ice.

He wrote:

I SEE. I AM VERY FAST AT SWIMMING.

The tern flew down to read this message. She tilted her head and scratched a response:

PENGUIINS CANNOT FLY. THIS RACE IS ONLY FOR FLYING.

Waddles considered this fact. Then he waddled to the edge of the iceberg, flapped his tiny wings with tremendous determination, and promptly belly-flopped into the cold blue sea.

I watched him surface, shaking water from his round shiny black eyes. He climbed back onto the ice using his oversized webbed feet and wrote:

YOU ARE CORRECT. I CANNOT FLY.

The arctic tern wrote back:

BUT YOU SWIM VERY WELL. I SAW YOU CATCH THOSE FISH.

This was the first compliment either of them had given the other. They both stared at the message for a long time, as if compliments were mysterious and required careful examination.

illustration.description: A chubby baby emperor penguin with fluffy grey-white down, round shiny black eyes, a tiny yellow beak, and oversized webbed feet climbs out of the cold blue sea onto the floating iceberg while an arctic tern flies overhead. Water drips from the penguin's feathers. Golden age illustration style with Arthur Rackham inspired detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale aesthetic, enchanted scenes with decorative borders.
illustration.alt: A penguin climbing out of water onto an iceberg while a tern flies above

pages.text:
On the third day, the messages became urgent.

From the arctic tern:

ICEBERG IS DRIFTING WRONG DIRECTION. RACE STARTS TOMORROW.

From Waddles:

I ALSO NEED TO GET HOME. MY COLONY IS THAT WAY. (He had drawn an arrow pointing east.)

From the tern:

RACE IS THAT WAY. (She had drawn an arrow pointing west.)

They both stared at these arrows, which pointed in exactly opposite directions.

Waddles scratched his message very carefully:

THIS IS A SERIOUS PROBLEM.

The arctic tern agreed:

VERY SERIOUS.

They spent the morning sitting on opposite sides of the iceberg, thinking about this serious problem. The iceberg continued drifting north, which was helpful to neither of them.

At noon, Waddles discovered something interesting. When he dove into the water and pushed against the iceberg with his flippers, the whole thing moved slightly.

He wrote:

I CAN PUSH ICEBERG. BUT ONLY A LITTLE.

The tern flew down to examine the scratches in the ice where Waddles had pushed. She wrote:

HMM. WHAT IF I HELP?

Waddles looked up at her with his round shiny black eyes.

HOW CAN YOU HELP? YOU ARE A BIRD.

I CAN FLY AHEAD AND FIND THE BEST CURRENT. TELL YOU WHERE TO PUSH.

This was such a sensible idea that Waddles fell over in surprise, landing in a pile of snow with only his tiny yellow beak visible.

illustration.description: A chubby baby emperor penguin with fluffy grey-white down, round shiny black eyes, a tiny yellow beak, and oversized webbed feet has fallen backwards into snow with only his beak showing, while an arctic tern stands nearby on the floating iceberg surrounded by cold blue sea. Golden age illustration style with Arthur Rackham inspired detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale aesthetic, timeless classic with decorative borders.
illustration.alt: A penguin fallen in snow with only his beak showing while a tern watches

pages.text:
The next morning, I watched their most important message exchange.

From the arctic tern:

I HAVE THOUGHT ABOUT THIS ALL NIGHT. WE NEED A PLAN.

From Waddles:

YES. VERY GOOD PLAN.

From the tern:

FIRST, I FLY UP HIGH. LOOK FOR WARM CURRENT GOING EAST.

From Waddles:

WHAT IS SECOND?

YOU SWIM BEHIND ICEBERG. PUSH TOWARD WARM CURRENT.

Waddles studied this plan with the seriousness of a professor examining a very difficult equation. He scratched:

WHAT ABOUT YOUR RACE?

The arctic tern stood very still for a moment. Then she wrote:

RACE IS IMPORTANT. BUT YOU NEED TO GET HOME TOO.

From Waddles:

WE COULD GET YOU CLOSE TO RACE FIRST. THEN GO TO MY COLONY.

The tern wrote:

THAT IS VERY KIND. BUT CURRENT ONLY GOES ONE WAY.

They both stared at this message. The cold blue sea lapped against the edges of the iceberg, making small splashing sounds.

Finally, Waddles wrote:

MAYBE GETTING HOME IS MORE IMPORTANT THAN WINNING RACE.

The tern considered this for a long time. When she finally responded, her message was very short:

YES. I THINK YOU ARE RIGHT.

Waddles nodded solemnly, as if they had just solved the most difficult puzzle in the world.

illustration.description: A chubby baby emperor penguin with fluffy grey-white down, round shiny black eyes, a tiny yellow beak, and oversized webbed feet sits next to an arctic tern, both looking at messages written in the snow on their floating iceberg. The cold blue sea surrounds them under a cloudy sky. Golden age illustration style with Arthur Rackham inspired detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale aesthetic, enchanted scenes with decorative borders.
illustration.alt: A penguin and tern looking at snow messages together on an iceberg

pages.text:
I have never seen such determined teamwork.

The arctic tern flew high above the cold blue sea, circling and diving to find the perfect current. Below, Waddles paddled with his oversized webbed feet and pushed with all his strength.

When the tern spotted the warm eastern current, she flew down and wrote:

LEFT! LEFT! BIG WARM CURRENT TO THE LEFT!

Waddles read the message, dove into the water, and pushed the iceberg with tremendous effort. Slowly, very slowly, the iceberg began to turn.

Then something wonderful happened. The warm current caught the iceberg and began pulling it steadily eastward.

Waddles climbed back onto the ice, shaking water from his fluffy grey-white down. The tern landed beside him, and they both watched the horizon change.

The arctic tern scratched in the snow:

WE DID IT. WE ARE GOING EAST.

Waddles wrote back:

YES. WE MAKE A GOOD TEAM.

I THINK SO TOO. EVEN THOUGH I WILL MISS THE RACE.

Waddles thought about this. Then he wrote something that surprised even me:

MAYBE WE JUST WON A DIFFERENT KIND OF RACE.

The arctic tern tilted her head.

WHAT KIND?

THE KIND WHERE EVERYONE GETS HOME.

The tern studied this message for a long moment. Then she wrote:

THAT IS THE BEST KIND OF RACE.

As the iceberg drifted steadily toward the penguin colony, both Waddles and the arctic tern fell asleep in the afternoon sun. Their final messages remained visible in the snow:

THANK YOU FOR HELPING ME.

THANK YOU FOR BEING A GOOD FRIEND.

I am just a message in a bottle, but even I know that some victories are more important than winning.

illustration.description: A chubby baby emperor penguin with fluffy grey-white down, round shiny black eyes, a tiny yellow beak, and oversized webbed feet sleeps peacefully next to an arctic tern on a floating iceberg drifting through the cold blue sea toward the horizon. Their messages remain visible in the snow around them. Golden age illustration style with Arthur Rackham inspired detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale aesthetic, timeless classic with decorative borders creating an enchanted scene.
illustration.alt: A penguin and tern sleeping together on an iceberg with messages in the snow

coverPrompt.description: A chubby baby emperor penguin with fluffy grey-white down, round shiny black eyes, a tiny yellow beak, and oversized webbed feet stands on a floating iceberg in a cold blue sea, holding a piece of paper. Golden age illustration style with Arthur Rackham inspired detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale aesthetic, decorative borders framing the enchanted scene.
coverPrompt.alt: A baby penguin on an iceberg holding a note

questions.question: Why couldn't Waddles and the arctic tern talk to each other at first?
choices: The tern thought penguins were too loud
questions.explanation: The arctic tern wrote that she didn't speak to penguins because they were too loud.

questions.question: What was the arctic tern training for?
questions.explanation: The tern was training for the Great Migration Race, where birds fly from ice to ice.

questions.question: What problem did Waddles and the tern discover?
choices: The iceberg was drifting the wrong way
questions.explanation: The iceberg was drifting north, but Waddles needed to go east and the tern needed to go west.

questions.question: How did they solve their problem?
choices: They worked together to steer the iceberg
questions.explanation: The tern flew ahead to find currents while Waddles pushed the iceberg from behind.

questions.question: What did the arctic tern give up to help Waddles?
questions.explanation: The tern chose to help Waddles get home instead of participating in her race.
